title: Irish stew
subtitle: Proper lamb neck, potato, and onion
excerpt: Lamb neck on the bone, layered with potato, onion, and a little thyme, then braised slowly in water until the stock thickens from the potatoes and the lamb falls from the bone. The simplest stew in the British tradition.
sourceNotes: Classic Irish dish documented in Florence Irwin, Irish Country Recipes (1937), which records the traditional version made with lamb neck, potato, and onion only, without additional vegetables or thickening. Further reference: Maura Laverty, Feasting Galore (1961). The dish is also mentioned in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. William Makepeace Thackeray, The Irish Sketch Book (1843): an early Victorian travel record praising Irish stew as cooked in country households.

content.text: Irish stew is one of the most minimal dishes in the British repertoire. Lamb neck, potato, onion, water, and time. That is the purist version. The recipe here adds a small amount of thyme and parsley. That is a common modern touch and does not change the character of the dish. Carrots appear in some versions and not others. Their use is a matter of regional preference, not authenticity.

Lamb neck is the correct cut: it has enough fat and connective tissue to braise down into something gelatinous and full-flavoured over two and a half hours. Shoulder can be used but will produce a slightly different result. The bone-in version is traditional and worth it for the flavour the bone gives to the broth.

items.name: Lamb neck
items.prepNote: bone-in, cut into 4 cm pieces by your butcher

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: floury variety, peeled and halved or quartered

content.text: Layer and bring to the boil

Layer the lamb neck pieces, {{onion}} sliced onions, and {{carrot}} carrot pieces (if using) in a Dutch oven or heavy-based pot. Tuck in the thyme and bay leaves. Season generously between layers. Add {{potato}} potato pieces on top. Pour over {{water}} of cold water, enough to come just below the potato layer; the potatoes will steam in the last part of the cook rather than being submerged.

Bring to the boil over medium heat and skim any grey foam from the surface. Cover tightly, reduce the heat to the lowest possible, and simmer for 2 hours. Check occasionally that the liquid has not evaporated; add a splash of hot water if needed. After 2 hours the lamb should be very tender, nearly falling from the bone. Remove the lid and cook for a further 20 to 30 minutes so the broth thickens slightly from the dissolving potato starch. Remove the thyme sprigs and bay leaves.
Taste and adjust the seasoning. Scatter the chopped parsley over the surface before serving. Serve in deep bowls with bread to mop up the broth.

items.fix: Continue cooking at a very gentle simmer. Never let Irish stew boil hard. The lower and slower, the better.
items.cause: The heat was too high and the collagen seized rather than dissolving
items.symptom: Lamb is still tough at 2 hours

items.fix: Add some of the potato earlier and some later next time. For a thicker broth, dissolving potatoes are not necessarily a problem.
items.cause: A floury variety cooked at too high a temperature
items.symptom: Potatoes have disintegrated into the broth

items.fix: Cook uncovered for longer to reduce. Alternatively, mash one or two potato pieces against the side of the pot to thicken the broth.
items.cause: Too much water relative to potatoes, or the potatoes were waxy and did not release starch

items.fix: Season generously at every stage. Irish stew relies on the lamb flavour alone; under-seasoned lamb produces an under-flavoured stew.
items.cause: Insufficient salt, or the lamb neck had little fat

content.text: Irish stew has a long history in pot cooking over an open fire. The pure version uses three things and water. Lamb, potato, and onion. Victorian travel writers and Irish country cooks both recorded that version. The simplest mix of what was on hand was the most reliable. Whether carrots belong has split Irish cooks for generations. The lamb-and-potato pair is the only fixed point. Some households use mutton instead of lamb. The flavour is stronger and suits the long braise even better. See the Sources block for the cookery writers who recorded the original.
